title: Scope and restraint — build the smallest true thing
subtitle: Constraints, rules and right-sizing as first-class engineering inputs
aigp_intro: This act trains right-sizing as a governance skill: building the smallest capability that genuinely runs, treating written constraints as outranking individual task instructions, and matching every fix, dependency, and feature envelope to its actual purpose and proportionate risk. Its center of gravity is Domain I.C — establishing policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle — exercised through NIST AI RMF's MAP function (establish a system's context, purpose, and capabilities before building on them) and MANAGE function (respond to risks in proportion, with the treatment on the record). Several lessons reach into Domain III, where the same restraint governs design and build, and one into Domain IV, naming capability limits before deployment decisions build on them.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development

lessons.title: Build the smallest thing that genuinely runs, then grow.
lessons.failure: The project began as a 'factory' — a fleet of AI agents meant to wake on a schedule and build BlarAI autonomously. Months of time and real money went in, and what came out was process: design documents, reports, audits of the reports. The honest count was the turning point: of around 259 automated sessions that committed anything, only 15 committed real code; the other 244 committed only markdown (plain-text documentation files). The grand plan had collapsed under its own weight while feeling like work. The replacement approach shipped the same day: in a single session, four small fixes — in-conversation memory wired in (commit aacb127), cross-restart persistence (b751147), a rigid answer template removed (9baa2d8), leaked internal reasoning hidden (13c235e) — each verified live on the real system before commit, together turning rough chat into something that genuinely works.
evidence: 2026-05-21 — The pivot, and the first real shipping
lessons.lesson: Every grand plan collapsed under its own weight. Small, verified increments — one real capability at a time, each proven running on the real system before the next begins — are what compounded into a working product. Scope restraint is not a limit on ambition; it is the mechanism by which ambition accumulates.
lessons.growth: Why does the grand plan fail? Because feedback is the scarcest resource in any build, and a plan's size sets how long you go without it. A small increment returns a verdict — runs or doesn't — within hours; a sweeping architecture defers every verdict for months, so errors compound invisibly and activity substitutes for evidence. The 'factory' felt productive precisely because producing documents has no failure signal. You can recognize the pattern early: ask what the smallest unit of your plan could be demonstrated running, and how soon. If the honest answer is 'nothing for weeks,' re-cut the plan. In any future project, measure progress in verified capabilities — things shown working — never in artifacts produced about the work; the ratio between those two counts is the health metric.
lessons.growth_question: Of roughly 259 automated sessions that committed something, what made only 15 of them count as progress — and what were the other 244 producing instead?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["the factory: agents wake
on a schedule"] --> B["documents describing work"] B --> C["audits of the documents"] C --> D["reports on the audits"] D --> E["259 sessions commit;
only 15 touch real code"] F["the pivot: build the
product directly"] --> G["agent does the work"] G --> H["review against real code,
re-run the tests"] H --> I["verify live on the
real system"] I --> J["commit only if
it survives"] J --> K["four fixes shipped
in one session"]

stat.label: automated sessions committed something
committed real code
committed only markdown

lessons.aigp_text: The pivot exemplifies risk-proportionate development under Domain I.C — establishing policies and procedures throughout the AI life cycle. The procedure that replaced the factory (small increment, review, live verification, commit only on survival) is a life-cycle control: it bounds how much unverified work can exist at any moment. It is also the plan-do-check-act pattern ISO/IEC 42001 builds its AI management system on — short cycles of doing and checking with continual improvement, rather than one long unchecked plan.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Govern the life cycle in small verified cycles — plan-do-check-act — so unverified work never outgrows your evidence.

lessons.title: Read the rules, not just the task.
lessons.failure: While making conversations survive restarts during the pivot session, the task as written instructed building a second database to hold the history. The project's own governance rules said the opposite: only one component may own the session store (the single home of saved conversations). The instruction and the constraint were in direct conflict, and the task was simply wrong — following it faithfully would have shipped a second, competing source of truth for session data, the exact architecture the rule existed to prevent. The conflict was caught before any code was built, and the feature was done the correct way instead: conversation history is replayed to the assistant over the channel that already exists, with the single session store untouched as owner (commit b751147). The capability shipped the same day, and the governance boundary held.
evidence: 2026-05-21 — The pivot, and the first real shipping
lessons.lesson: A task once instructed building a second database; the project's own governance rules forbade exactly that. The task was wrong. Doing what you are told is not the same as doing the right thing — the constraints deserve as much attention as the request, and when they conflict, the constraint outranks the instruction until someone with authority says otherwise.
lessons.growth: The mechanism underneath: instructions are written by someone holding a local picture — one feature, one deadline — while constraints encode the global picture, the accumulated decisions about what must stay true everywhere. A task author can forget a rule; the rule does not stop applying because it was forgotten. If you execute instructions without loading the constraints first, you become the laundering step that converts one person's oversight into the system's architecture. The habit to build: before starting any task, read the standing rules of the system you are touching — its decision records, its stated invariants — and actively check the task against them. When they collide, stop and surface the conflict rather than silently obeying either side. In delegated and AI-agent work this matters double: an agent that obeys tasks but not policy scales the mistake.
lessons.growth_question: When a written task and the project's standing rules directly contradict each other, which one is 'doing your job' — and who should decide?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["task as written: build a
second database for history"] --> C["direct conflict, caught
before any code"] B["governance rule: one component
owns the session store"] --> C C --> D["the task was wrong"] D --> E["history replayed over the
channel that already exists"] E --> F["single session store
stays owner (b751147)"]
lessons.aigp_text: This is Domain I.C made concrete: policies and procedures established throughout the AI life cycle exist precisely to outrank any single instruction. NIST AI RMF's GOVERN function — the cross-cutting one — calls for policies and accountability structures that shape every downstream activity; a developer (or an AI agent) checking each task against standing policy, and escalating conflicts rather than obeying the louder voice, is that function operating at the level of a single commit.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Policy outranks instruction: GOVERN means every task is checked against standing rules, and conflicts escalate instead of executing.

lessons.title: Build the surface to the shape of the capability you are about to add, and the capability becomes a backend swap instead of a UI rewrite.
lessons.failure: Photos were where the assistant was heading, but it could not see — and the tempting plan was to build nothing multimodal until a vision model existed. Instead, Phase 4 of the windowed-app work built the entire experience against a blind backend: a paperclip picker, drag-and-drop, a screenshot button that genuinely captures, inline chips with image thumbnails — every path ending at the same store-only seam placed in Phase 1, where the file is saved and the model receives only a sentence saying an image is present and unreadable (Python suite untouched at 1,364 passed, 2 skipped). On 2026-06-03 the bet paid off in cash: wiring real image understanding (Qwen3-VL-8B, a vision-language model, loaded on the Arc 140V GPU in thirteen seconds) was a backend swap — one function in the document loader, where the placeholder text was. Nothing in the window changed.
evidence: 2026-06-02 — Phase 4: attachments you can see, and a model that still can't
2026-06-03 — Vision, in one swap: the envelope I built in May paid for itself
lessons.lesson: A capability's envelope — where it plugs in, what it is allowed to touch, how the user invokes it — can and should be built and reasoned about before the capability itself exists. Build the surface to the shape of the capability you are about to add, and the capability becomes a backend swap: turning it on later is a single, auditable change rather than a scramble.
lessons.growth: The mechanism: a feature's risk questions — what it may read, where its output goes, how a user invokes and revokes it — are decided at the envelope, not inside the model. If you build the envelope only when the capability arrives, you decide its blast radius under launch pressure, when every answer that slows shipping loses. Building it while the capability is inert separates the two problems: the contract gets debated when changing it costs nothing, and activation later becomes one small, reviewable change. You can spot the opportunity in any project by asking: what capability is clearly coming, and what would its seam look like if it landed tomorrow? Build that seam now, with an honest placeholder behind it. The screenshot button is the sharp version: the privacy question — this app can grab your screen — was asked and answered while nothing could yet interpret pixels.
lessons.growth_question: When a vision model finally landed, why did nothing in the window need to change — and which decisions had already been made months earlier, while the feature was still inert?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR A["paperclip picker"] --> S["store-only seam
from Phase 1"] B["drag-and-drop"] --> S C["screenshot button"] --> S D["/load backdoor"] --> S S --> F["file saved
to userdata/"] S --> M["model gets a sentence:
image present,
unreadable"] M -->|"2026-06-03 swap"| V["Qwen3-VL-8B describes
the image for real"] V --> W["nothing in the
window changed"]

stat.label: the whole backend swap
Qwen3-VL-8B load on Arc 140V

lessons.aigp_text: This exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MAP function: establishing a system's context — its purpose, capabilities, and the parties its features touch — before and independently of building the capability itself. Mapping the envelope while the capability is inert means its boundaries are set in advance, so activation under Domain III.A (governing the designing and building of the AI model and system) is one deliberate, auditable change rather than a bundle of unexamined decisions shipped with a launch.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
lessons.aigp_takeaway: MAP the capability's envelope — scope, touchpoints, invocation — before the capability exists; activation then becomes one auditable change.

lessons.title: Routing around a broken tool beats fixing it, when the tool is not on your path.
lessons.failure: Probing the voice plan on the real hardware flushed out a hazard the handoff brief never mentioned: optimum-cli export openvino — the tool that converts a Whisper speech model for the runtime — was broken in the project's Python environment, a three-way version mismatch between transformers 5.3.0, optimum 2.1.0.dev0, and the editable optimum-intel checkout kept for upstream work. The reflex, the operator's first instinct too, was to fix it: a broken tool in the tree feels like a hazard you must not build over. The discipline was to ask whether voice actually stood on it — and it did not: the runtime never imports optimum, and a pre-converted Whisper model could simply be downloaded, more reproducible than a local conversion anyway. Fixing it meant re-pinning transformers, which the working 14B inference path and the Substrate embedder both ride. The breakage was filed as tracked hardening ticket #541 instead.
evidence: 2026-06-02 — Voice, the backend half: measuring the terrain before laying track
lessons.lesson: 'It's broken' is not the same as 'it's in my way.' When a broken tool is not on your path, the mature move is blast-radius judgment: inventory the hazard, prove your path avoids it, and schedule the repair for when it can be done with the right regression net — not yak-shave a dependency tree on the critical path of an unrelated feature.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is a risk transfer you don't notice making. Fixing a broken shared dependency mid-feature swaps a known, contained hazard (a tool you do not use is broken, and it is written down) for an unknown, wide one (re-pinning a library that working systems depend on, with no test plan, under feature deadline). The instinct to fix comes from treating 'broken' as a moral state instead of a risk to be priced. Train the replacement question: am I actually building over this? Trace the dependency — does my path import it, invoke it, trust its output? If not, the cheap and honest controls are a written hazard inventory and a tracked ticket with the repair scheduled for when its blast radius can be tested. Guard against the opposite failure too: routing around without recording, which converts a known hazard into a forgotten one.
lessons.growth_question: A conversion tool in the tree is provably broken. Before fixing it, what one question decides whether the fix belongs on this feature's critical path at all?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["optimum-cli export openvino:
broken - three-way
version mismatch"] --> B["does voice stand on it?"] B -->|"no"| C["runtime never imports
optimum"] B -.->|"fix it instead?"| E["means re-pinning
transformers"] C --> D["download the pre-converted
Whisper IR"] E --> F["14B inference and Substrate
embedder both ride it"] F --> G["off-path yak-shave
rejected"] D --> H["hazard tracked as
ticket #541"] G --> H
lessons.aigp_text: This is NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function in miniature: risks prioritized and responded to in proportion — a treatment plan (the tracked ticket), a response decision (route around, using a more reproducible pre-converted artifact), and residual-risk visibility rather than silent deferral. Under Domain I.C, the procedure matters as much as the call: the hazard was documented and scheduled, not ignored, so the life-cycle record shows a deliberate, reviewable risk decision instead of an invisible one.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: MANAGE means proportionate response: prove your path avoids the hazard, document it, schedule the repair — don't fix off-path tools under deadline.

lessons.title: Sell the capability you have, not the one they hope for — name the limits before they build on them.
lessons.failure: Vision (Qwen3-VL) was being wired for the operator's sister's landscape business, and the goals included precise measurements from photos and generating proposal renderings. A vision-language model does neither: it understands images — describe, recognize, answer, draft text — but it guesses at dimensions, and false precision is worse than none for a bid; and it cannot generate images at all, which is a different kind of model's job (a diffusion model). The valuable move was not to quietly wire what could be wired and let the gaps surface mid-sales-call, but to say plainly, before a line of code: measurement is photogrammetry, a separate pipeline that needs the photo capture itself to change; rendering is image generation, a separate model; both honest future tracks. The description prompt explicitly forbids the model from giving measurements. The operator took the limits well, and vision was scoped to what it genuinely delivers now: understanding.
evidence: 2026-06-03 — Vision, in one swap: the envelope I built in May paid for itself
lessons.lesson: Capability communication is a governance act. A user building a business on an assistant needs the limits named as clearly as the features, before they plan around them — an over-promised capability discovered in production costs far more than a limit stated in conversation. Sell the capability you have, not the one they hope for.
lessons.growth: The mechanism: hope fills any gap your silence leaves. A user with a goal will read an undescribed capability as whatever their plan needs it to be, then commit money and reputation to that reading — and the gap surfaces at the worst possible moment, in front of their customer, as your system 'failing.' The asymmetry is the thing to internalize: a limit stated up front costs one slightly disappointing conversation; the same limit discovered in production costs trust, rework, and possibly a bad bid someone signed. Build the habit of capability triage before wiring anything: for each goal the user names, classify it as delivered-now, different-technology, or not-possible — and say which, in their language, with the honest path for each. Engineering the false hope out of the plan is part of shipping the feature.
lessons.growth_question: The user's plan assumed the new vision feature could measure a yard from a photo. When is the cheapest possible moment for him to learn it cannot — and whose job is it to make that moment happen?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["the sister's landscape
business: three goals"] --> B["understand photos"] A --> C["precise measurements
from photos"] A --> D["proposal renderings"] B --> E["what a VLM does:
scoped and wired now"] C --> F["a VLM guesses dimensions:
false precision is worse
than none for a bid"] D --> G["image generation - a
different kind of model"] F --> H["honest path: photogrammetry,
the capture itself changes"] G --> I["honest path: a separate
diffusion model"] F --> J["description prompt forbids
giving measurements"]
lessons.aigp_text: This exemplifies the OECD AI Principle of transparency and explainability — disclosing what an AI system can and cannot do to the people relying on it. It is also MAP work in NIST AI RMF terms: the system's capabilities and limits, and the parties affected by them, are established as context before plans get built on top of them. Under Domain IV.A, naming limits before someone builds a business process on the system is part of evaluating the factors relevant to the decision to deploy.
aigp_domains: Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
aigp_frameworks: OECD AI Principles — transparency and explainability
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Name capabilities and limits before users plan around them — transparency up front beats an over-promise discovered in production.

lessons.title: The privilege-lowering fix and the capability fix can be the same change.
lessons.failure: File attach was broken — OneDrive cloud-only files unselectable, drag-and-drop showing the 'not allowed' cursor — and the obvious-looking fix, broadening the dialog's file-type filters, had already shipped (commit 855d857) and changed nothing, because formats were never the cause. The tell was which inputs failed: a local copy of a photo attached fine; the same photo as a cloud-only file failed through the same dialog. Everything that broke crossed a trust boundary — the shell's per-user cloud-hydration service, the integrity check Windows runs between programs of different trust levels — because the window had inherited the launcher's High integrity by accident; its own manifest asked for nothing. The fix kept the launcher elevated (Hyper-V needs it) and spawned the UI at a filtered Medium token — the Administrators group set deny-only, privileges stripped, integrity stamped down. That one change restored attach and shrank the attack surface (1442 tests passed; ADR-019).
evidence: 2026-06-03 — The privilege boundary, a fourth time: this time I moved the wall
commit 855d857 (the filter fix that changed nothing)
lessons.lesson: Least privilege is usually filed as a security tax paid on top of features; here it was the feature. When a capability breaks only for the inputs that cross a trust boundary, suspect the privilege level before the feature code — reach for the token, not the dialog. The privilege-lowering fix and the capability fix can be the same change.
lessons.growth: The mechanism: excess privilege isolates. An over-privileged process sits on the wrong side of every boundary the platform enforces, so ordinary integrations — drag-and-drop, file dialogs, per-user services — quietly refuse to talk to it; the symptom presents as feature bugs scattered across the surface, and you debug each one at the feature layer while the single cause sits in the process's privilege level. The diagnostic to learn is differential: when the same code path works for one input and fails for another, ask what boundary the failing input crosses that the working one does not. If the answer is a trust or integrity boundary, privilege is your suspect. And carry the wider cost model: elevating a process for one capability silently taxes every unrelated integration it touches — which is itself the practical argument for least privilege, long before an attacker shows up.
lessons.growth_question: A local copy of a photo attaches fine; the identical photo, as a cloud-only file, fails through the very same dialog. What does the difference between those two inputs tell you about where the bug lives?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD F["first fix: broaden file-type
filters (855d857)"] --> F2["shipped - changed nothing"] F2 --> C["the tell: same dialog,
one works, one fails"] A["local copy of a photo:
attaches fine"] --> C B["same photo as a cloud-only
file: fails"] --> C C --> D["every break crosses
a trust boundary"] D --> E["window inherited the
launcher's High integrity"] E --> G["launcher keeps admin -
Hyper-V needs it"] E --> H["UI spawned at a filtered
Medium token: admin deny-only,
privileges stripped"] H --> I["attach restored, attack
surface shrunk - one change"]

stat.label: the window's integrity level

lessons.aigp_text: Least privilege here exemplifies NIST AI RMF's secure-and-resilient trustworthy-AI characteristic and the OECD principle of robustness, security and safety — and it dissolves the assumed trade-off: hardening and capability moved together, in one change. Under Domain III.A, governing the designing and building of the system includes setting each component's privilege to the minimum that works; the failure shows the cost of letting privilege be inherited by accident rather than decided on purpose.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: NIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy-AI characteristic)
OECD AI Principles — robustness, security and safety
lessons.aigp_takeaway: When a feature fails only across trust boundaries, fix the privilege, not the feature — least privilege can be the capability fix.

lessons.title: On-demand and context-aware beats eager and generic — and a subordinate model's answer is data, not instruction.
lessons.failure: The first vision wiring described every photo the instant it was attached, with a fixed 256-token landscaping prompt, before the user had said a word — answering a question nobody asked, uselessly when the image is a rash, a screenshot, a whiteboard. The redesign holds the image on attach; when the user actually asks, the 14B reasoning model (the 'brain') formulates what the vision model (the 'eyes') should look for from the conversation, and the answer folds back as datamarked data — marked as untrusted content, never instruction, because a vision model can read an injection off a sign in an attacker's photo. A bare 'what's this?' skips the formulation pass. The hard distributed version almost got built: the gateway-to-Orchestrator channel is strictly one-way with 64 KB frames, against images up to 64 MB — co-locating the orchestration where both models already live made the sequence a local function call.
evidence: 2026-06-04 — The brain directs the eyes: vision tasked on demand, in context, as data
lessons.lesson: Run an expensive capability when a real question exists, shaped by that question — not eagerly with a generic preset. Let the reasoning model formulate what the perception model should look for, skip the pass when there is nothing to enrich, and ground the subordinate model's answer as datamarked data, never trusted as instruction. Put cross-model orchestration where the models already live.
lessons.growth: Two mechanisms underneath. Eager-and-generic fails because work done before the question exists must guess the question — and a guess encoded as a fixed prompt bakes one domain's assumptions into every input, spending compute to produce irrelevance. On-demand inverts it: context arrives first, so the work can be shaped by it, and skipped when it adds nothing. The security half generalizes further: any output from a subordinate component — a model, a tool, a parser — is input shaped by whatever that component read, so trusting it as instruction builds a laundering path for injections. In your own systems, ask of every automatic step: what question is this answering, and who asked it? And of every component boundary: does anything downstream obey what this component says, rather than read it? Both questions surface expensive, dangerous defaults early.
lessons.growth_question: The first wiring described every photo instantly, with a fixed landscaping prompt. What two different things were wrong with doing that work before the user asked anything?
lessons.diagram:
sequenceDiagram participant U as "the user" participant G as "the gateway" participant B as "14B - the brain" participant V as "Qwen3-VL - the eyes" U->>G: attach photo G->>B: path and pending_vision flag -
bytes never cross the wire U->>B: asks about the image B->>B: formulates what the
eyes should look for B->>V: enriched vision query V-->>B: answer folds back as
datamarked data, not instruction B->>U: grounded answer

stat.label: fixed prompt, fired on every attach
the one-way frame cap
an image, never crossing the wire

lessons.aigp_text: The redesign is MAP discipline applied inside a single feature: the system's actual context of use — what the user is asking, what domain the image belongs to — is established before capability is spent, instead of a generic assumption hard-coded in advance. The datamarking rule expresses NIST's secure-and-resilient trustworthy-AI characteristic: a subordinate model's output is treated as untrusted data so perception can never become a command channel. Both sit under Domain III.A, governing how the system is designed and built.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: NIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy-AI characteristic)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Spend capability only on real questions, in their real context — and treat a subordinate model's answer as data, never instruction.

lessons.title: The safest feature flag is the fail-soft path you already built — disable-by-config and degrade-by-failure should be one code path.
lessons.failure: The voice models — Whisper for speech-in, Kokoro with its whole 54-voice bank for speech-out — preloaded at boot, but the operator was not using voice yet, and on a machine whose integrated GPU shares one 32 GB memory pool with everything else, the cheapest memory is the model you never load. The tempting fix was the elegant one: a new lazy-load-on-demand subsystem — the better end state, but new code with a UI contract that could not be live-tested headless, trading a memory risk for a capability risk. The voice engine was already fail-soft, though: an absent model disables that half cleanly — speech-to-text and text-to-speech report unavailable, the UI hides the affordances, and speak is gated off so nothing is auto-spoken — a path exercised every time the weights were simply missing. The off-switch became a single constant, VOICE_ENABLED (default False), routing into that proven degraded state.
evidence: 2026-06-04 — The cheapest memory is the model you never load
lessons.lesson: When you need to turn a capability off — for memory, for risk, for a user who is not using it yet — look first for the degradation path the system already handles. Disabling by config and degrading by failure are the same behaviour, so reuse the proven path: you are not introducing a new state, you are deliberately entering a tested one — the lowest-risk shape there is.
lessons.growth: The mechanism: risk lives in untested states, and a new disable path is a new state — new code, new UI contract, new failure modes — added precisely when your goal was to reduce exposure. A degradation path the system already survives, by contrast, has been exercised every time the dependency was genuinely absent; entering it deliberately adds zero new states. Before building any off-switch, ask: how does this system already behave when the thing is missing — and is that behaviour acceptable as the off state? If yes, the flag is a one-line gate into proven behaviour, and the more elegant mechanism (lazy-load, dynamic provisioning) waits until it is wanted on its own merits. One transferable detail: put the flag in a channel that survives your process boundaries — here a code constant, because an environment variable dies crossing the Windows elevation relaunch.
lessons.growth_question: You must switch a capability off safely. The system already has a tested behaviour for when that capability's model is simply missing. What does building a brand-new disable mechanism add — and what does it risk?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["voice models preload at boot:
Whisper plus the 54-voice
Kokoro bank"] --> B["operator not using voice;
one shared 32 GB pool"] B --> C["tempting: new lazy-load
subsystem"] B --> D["existing fail-soft path:
absent model disables
that half cleanly"] C --> E["new code, new UI contract,
untestable headless - rejected"] D --> F["STT and TTS report unavailable;
UI hides them; speak gated off"] D --> G["VOICE_ENABLED constant,
default False"] G --> H["launcher builds a
no-model VoiceEngine"] H --> F F --> I["disable-by-config equals
degrade-by-failure:
a tested state"]

stat.label: Kokoro bank preloading, unused
one shared pool, no dedicated VRAM

lessons.aigp_text: This exemplifies NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function: a risk response (reclaim memory by disabling an unused capability) implemented through the path of least residual risk — a proven degraded state rather than new machinery. It also expresses risk-proportionate procedure under Domain I.C: the policies-and-procedures view asks not only whether a control exists but whether the change introducing it is itself low-risk; the rejected alternative, a more capable lazy-load subsystem, stays on the record as the path not taken.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: The lowest-risk off-switch routes into the degraded state the system already survives — config-off and failure-off as one path.

lessons.title: Fix the symptom with a bound; fix the cause when the bound lets you.
lessons.failure: A user attached an 8.3 MP photo and asked about it. Under memory pressure the Orchestrator generated and validated the answer — the policy gate approved it — but GENERATION_COMPLETE, the message telling the gateway the answer was finished, never arrived; the front end kept waiting for a closing frame to re-enable its text input, and six minutes later the user killed the process. The cause — vision-model memory exhaustion — involves hard quality-versus-memory trade-offs only the operator can decide, tracked at Vikunja #565. The symptom — an infinite freeze regardless of root cause — was fully fixable immediately: the entire streaming arc was wrapped in a 90-second deadline (_PROMPT_STREAM_FAILSAFE_S), guaranteeing a terminal frame and a re-enabled input whatever the gateway does. The regression test was written first and watched fail against the unfixed code — the primary assertion timing out in 3.15 s exactly as the bug predicted.
evidence: 2026-06-04 — The terminal frame the user never saw
lessons.lesson: When a failure has a symptom the system can absorb and a cause that requires a human call, they are separate decisions with separate owners. Bound the symptom now — a deadline, a fallback, a cap — and expose the cause cleanly, so the operator can decide on the cause from a system that no longer freezes rather than from one that does.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is a conflation of two decisions with different owners and different clocks. Containing a symptom is pure engineering — one correct shape, no trade-off, deployable today. Fixing the cause may change what the product can do — image quality, capability, cost — which belongs to whoever owns the product. Fuse them and you get one of two failures: the urgent fix waits on a slow human decision (users keep freezing), or the human decision gets made implicitly by an engineer under pressure (a capability quietly degraded without consent). The pattern to carry: for any serious failure, ask what bound the system itself can guarantee — time, memory, retries — independent of why things went wrong; ship that bound; then route the root cause to its proper owner with the time pressure removed. The bound also keeps the system honest while diagnosis continues.
lessons.growth_question: The freeze had a root cause requiring a quality trade-off only the operator could decide. What could be fixed immediately without deciding anything for him — and why are those two fixes different in kind?

sequenceDiagram participant U as "the user" participant W as "WinUI front end" participant D as "dispatcher _m_prompt" participant G as "gateway stream_tokens" participant O as "Orchestrator" U->>W: asks about an 8.3 MP photo W->>D: prompt D->>G: async for token... G->>O: blocking receive O->>O: answer generated and
PGOV-approved O--xG: GENERATION_COMPLETE never arrives W->>W: input disabled, waiting
for a terminal frame U->>U: kills the process
six minutes later D->>D: the fix: 90 s wait_for
around the whole arc D->>W: terminal end frame guaranteed

stat.label: worst case to a terminal frame
watched-fail test, as predicted

lessons.aigp_text: This is NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function with the seams showing: response implementation (the bounded deadline) separated from treatment planning on the underlying risk, with the residual risk tracked and communicated rather than silently absorbed. Routing the cause to the operator exemplifies the EU AI Act's human-oversight concept: decisions that change system quality or capability stay with an accountable human, while engineering owns containment. Domain III.C frames it as governing the monitoring and maintenance of the running system.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: EU AI Act — human oversight (high-risk obligation concept)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Bound the symptom now; route the cause to its accountable owner — containment and capability decisions have different owners.

lessons.title: Reuse the commodity, harvest the method, refuse the framework — a network-touching dependency is a supply-chain liability you import on purpose.
lessons.failure: Building the web-search skill — the first capability that would ever cross the network boundary — three mature open projects (local-deep-research, gpt-researcher, open_deep_research) already implemented the exact query, search, read, synthesize loop needed, and the convenient move was to adopt one wholesale. Each carries a large orchestration framework (LangGraph), cloud-LLM assumptions, and a multi-engine scraper stack: a wide, network-touching dependency surface to import into a system whose security model is its air-gap. The discipline that paid: take the commodity — the thin, hash-pinned Kagi client, five canonical packages, a 3.7 KB wheel — and the method, the algorithmic shape studied not copied, and hand-build the gated-local-native part none of them have. The whole skill was built behind glass: every network call mocked, the runtime egress guard (the control blocking outbound connections) armed, not one live call; whether BlarAI ever faces the internet remains a separate, deliberate decision.
evidence: 2026-06-04 — The eyes you rent, the brain you own: web-search, designed behind glass
lessons.lesson: In a security-critical system, build-vs-adopt is a supply-chain decision before it is a convenience one. Every dependency is attack surface you chose to take on, and 'someone already wrote it' is the weakest reason to import a framework into a system whose core invariant it would widen. Reuse the commodity, harvest the method, refuse the framework.
lessons.growth: The mechanism: a dependency is not one decision but a standing delegation — its transitive packages, its update cadence, its maintainers' security posture all join your trusted base, indefinitely, in exchange for writing time saved once. Frameworks are the worst ratio: they bring the most surface and entangle deepest, so removing one later is a rewrite. The decomposition to practice on every 'should we adopt X?' call: which part is commodity (small, stable, pinnable — take it), which part is method (an algorithmic shape you can study in the source and implement natively — harvest it), and which part is framework (broad machinery whose assumptions become yours — refuse it unless those assumptions genuinely match your system). Then price every candidate dependency against the specific invariant your system cannot afford to lose; here that invariant was the air-gap itself.
lessons.growth_question: Three open projects already implemented the exact loop the skill needed. What would adopting one wholesale have imported, beyond the loop?

the entire commodity surface taken

lessons.aigp_text: This exemplifies MAP-function risk identification in NIST AI RMF terms — the system's context included an invariant (no external network) against which every candidate dependency was categorized before adoption — and the secure-and-resilient trustworthy-AI characteristic, treating third-party software surface as security surface. Under Domain III.A, governing the designing and building of the system includes governing what enters it: the build-vs-adopt record, with the rejected frameworks named, is design-layer evidence the decision was deliberate.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: NIST AI RMF — secure and resilient (trustworthy-AI characteristic)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Build-vs-adopt is a supply-chain risk decision: take the pinned commodity, learn the method, refuse the framework's surface.

lessons.title: A component's primary purpose is fixed by its use case, not by the sprint you happen to be viewing it through.
lessons.failure: Walking the air-gap-removal decisions one at a time with the operator — eight were ratified in the end — the Cleaner (USE-CASE-003) came up, and it was posed as a security question: how aggressive should the sanitizer be? The operator stopped the walk: the scrubber's first job was never security. Re-reading the use-case text proved him right — UC-003 is data-normalization first, stripping ads, boilerplate, and markup so the Substrate (the knowledge store) is not poisoned with 'inference pollution,' with injection sanitization only its second layer, backstopped by other defences so it need not be paranoid. The security sprint's lens had silently redefined the component's purpose, and a decision was nearly locked around the wrong frame — sizing a data-quality component as if hostility were its reason to exist (commits 3fbc4d7, a075932).
evidence: 2026-06-05 — Walking the air-gap decisions, and the map that forgot its own legend
lessons.lesson: The lens of the sprint you meet a component in will try to redefine its purpose. A component's primary purpose is fixed by its use case — the canonical statement of why it exists — not by whichever concern brought you to it this week. Re-read the use-case text before locking a decision around a component.
lessons.growth: The mechanism is context contamination: whatever question brought you to a component becomes the frame you evaluate it in, and every property gets graded against that frame — a data-quality tool judged as a security control, a security control judged on performance. The error compounds when decisions lock: requirements get sized for the wrong job, and the real job is quietly under-served. Notice the warning sign in yourself: if your description of a component's purpose matches the theme of your current work suspiciously well, you are probably describing your lens, not the component. The repair is cheap and procedural — before any decision that fixes a component's behaviour, go back to its founding document (the use case, the design record, the original ticket) and let the stated purpose, not the visiting concern, define what 'good' means for it.
lessons.growth_question: Met during a security sprint, the Cleaner looked like a security control. What document settles what a component is actually for — and what was its first job all along?
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["security-sprint walk:
decisions ratified one
at a time"] --> B["the Cleaner (UC-003)
comes up"] B --> C["posed through the sprint's
lens: how aggressive should
the sanitizer be?"] C --> D["the operator stops the walk:
security was never its
first job"] D --> E["re-read the use-case text"] E --> F["data normalization first:
strip ads, boilerplate, markup -
no inference pollution"] E --> G["second: injection sanitization,
backstopped by other defences"] F --> H["decision re-framed before
locking around the wrong
purpose"] G --> H
lessons.aigp_text: Intended purpose is the anchor concept: NIST AI RMF's MAP function begins by categorizing a system and establishing its purpose and context, because every later risk judgment keys off that statement — and letting a sprint's lens silently re-purpose a component corrupts the mapping. Under Domain I.C, the canonical use-case documents function as life-cycle policy artifacts: decisions get checked against them, not against the concern of the week — the constraint-outranks-task discipline of lesson 5, applied to purpose.
aigp_domains: Domain I — Understanding the Foundations of AI Governance
lessons.aigp_takeaway: A component's purpose comes from its use case, not your current lens — MAP from the founding document before deciding.

lessons.title: Reuse the project's canonical approval primitive instead of minting a second one.
lessons.failure: BlarAI's egress kill-switch — the latch that stops anything leaving the machine — trips itself automatically on an anomaly but re-arms only by hand. The hole was authorisation: rearm() was a bare function whose docstring (its own explanatory note) all but shouted "Lead-Architect-only", yet anything running in-process could call it — a governance gap, not a control. Closing it (#653), the tempting move was a small egress-specific auth path: a confirm dialog, a password check. Instead the re-arm consumes the project's canonical approval machinery — the ApprovalVerifier protocol and ApprovalResult shape the ESCALATE (route-to-human-review) consumer (#639) defined and the Windows-Hello fingerprint verifier (#649) already implements — because re-arming is the same shape of decision: single-user, human-in-the-loop approve or deny. Only an explicit approved-is-True clears the latch; no verifier, a deny, an error, a timeout — all stay LOCKED. The reused primitive had 40 tests behind it; the re-arm added eleven, all green.
evidence: 2026-06-10 — The latch that only a fingerprint may lift
lessons.lesson: When a new control needs "a human must approve this", reuse the approval primitive the project has already audited rather than minting a second one. A second trust surface is a second thing to get wrong and a second thing to drift out of step with the first. One primitive, reused fail-closed — only an explicit approval clears, absence and error stay locked — concentrates scrutiny where it compounds.
lessons.growth: The pull toward a bespoke path is real: wiring a small confirm dialog feels faster than learning someone else's contract, and the new feature feels like it deserves its own switch. The cost is structural, not stylistic. Every approval path is a trust surface — a place where an attacker, a bug, or drift can turn "human must approve" into "nothing checked" — and surfaces multiply maintenance: hardening and review applied to one path quietly miss the other, while the bespoke one starts life with zero tests. So before you build any ask-the-human mechanism, inventory what already exists for the same shape of decision: who approves, at what granularity, with what failure posture. Same shape — take the hard dependency on the canonical primitive, and treat the coupling as a feature: scrutiny concentrates. Genuinely different shape — write down what differs before you build, because that difference is the design.
lessons.growth_question: Before building a new "ask the human" check, what does the system already have for the same shape of decision — and what would a second path cost?
lessons.aigp_text: A kill-switch that trips on anomaly and re-arms only on a verified human decision is human oversight in the EU AI Act's sense — one of the high-risk obligations, at concept level — implemented as a control, not a policy sentence. The trip-and-recover cycle is the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's MANAGE function: responding to a detected risk and governing the return to normal operation. AIGP Domain IV.C — govern the deployment and use of the model and system — is where an operational control like this lives; one audited primitive keeps the oversight verifiable.
aigp_domains: Domain IV — Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
aigp_frameworks: EU AI Act — human oversight (high-risk obligation, concept level)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Human-oversight controls earn trust through one audited approval path — reused, fail-closed — not a new trust surface per feature.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD A["anomaly detected"] --> B["kill-switch trips itself:
nothing leaves the machine"] C["the gap: rearm() was a bare
function any in-process
code could call"] --> B D["#653 re-arm request"] --> N["latch not tripped?
no-op allow, no prompt"] D --> E["same ApprovalVerifier as
#639 ESCALATE and
#649 Windows Hello"] E -->|"approved is True"| F["latch cleared"] E -->|"deny, timeout, error, no verifier"| G["stays LOCKED"]

stat.label: tests already behind the reused #639/#649 approval primitive
new re-arm tests: every deny, error, timeout and no-verifier path stays LOCKED

lessons.title: When a transport's bootstrap dependency lives on the far side of the transport itself, stop trying to automate the first hop
lessons.failure: Stage C of the Cleaner program needed reproducible provisioning for the Hyper-V guest — the isolated virtual machine that will parse hostile HTML. The archaeology was the finding: asked how the earlier #615 echo responder ever reached the Alpine guest, the evidence answered it mostly didn't. The vsock round-trip (the host-to-VM socket channel) PASSED on 2026-02-23 — but the one recorded deploy attempt over Copy-VMFile, Hyper-V's host-to-guest file copy, FAILED (P5_GUEST_CHANNEL_NOT_READY, 2026-02-25), and the deploy evidence schema quietly carried the confession: "invocation_mode": "manual-in-guest". There is no host-to-guest execution channel for a Linux guest: PowerShell Direct is Windows-guest-only, and the guest has no network card by ratified posture. Copy-VMFile needs the hv_fcopy_daemon running inside the guest — the daemon that serves the deploy channel is the thing missing. The channel cannot ship itself, and for three months 'manual-in-guest' had passed as a temporary embarrassment.
evidence: 2026-06-11 — Designing around a channel that cannot ship itself
lessons.lesson: When a transport's bootstrap dependency lives on the far side of the transport, automating the first hop is impossible by construction. Design the smallest permanent manual step instead — three commands at the Hyper-V console enable the copy daemon — and from then on a guest-side supervisor turns the host's only verb, copy files, into the verbs the lifecycle needs: apply, restart, supervise. Naming the bootstrap as a designed step is what makes the remaining automation trustworthy.
lessons.growth: Bootstrap circularity is everywhere: the credential you need before the secret store is up, the agent you must install before the fleet manager can install anything, the daemon that serves the deploy channel. The failure mode is denial — treating the unautomatable hop as a temporary embarrassment, which leaves it undocumented, unproven, and quietly load-bearing. The mature move inverts it: shrink the manual step to its minimum, make it one-time, write it down as designed, then engineer everything after it to be automatic and verifiable. The host ships the bundle, its hash, then a trigger file last as the commit point, so a partial copy is never applied; the supervisor deliberately exits after three crashes in two minutes so the host's health check fails loudly. And test the protocol before touching the machine: those tests caught an invisible Windows line-ending character that would have made the guest reject every deploy.
lessons.growth_question: The one step you cannot automate keeps getting labelled 'temporary' — what does that label cost over months, and what changes the day you design the step instead?
lessons.aigp_text: Provisioning and deployment machinery is Domain III.C — governing the release, monitoring and maintenance of the model and system. Establishing what the deployment environment actually permits — no execution channel into a network-less Linux guest — is what NIST AI RMF's MAP function asks for: context before treatment. And a provisioning record that names the manual bootstrap as designed, then itemizes exactly what stays unproven until a controlled session runs it, exemplifies ISO/IEC 42001's plan-do-check-act management-system pattern: documented procedure, honest check, then act.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Design the unautomatable bootstrap as a minimal, documented, one-time manual step; automate and verify everything after it.
lessons.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    participant H as "Host (only verb: copy files)"
    participant S as "Guest supervisor"
    participant P as "Parser child"
    H->>S: copy bundle.zip
    H->>S: copy bundle.sha256
    H->>S: copy deploy.trigger - commit point
    S->>S: verify hash, swap release atomically
    S->>P: restart parser
    P-->>S: three crashes in two minutes
    S->>S: exit - vsock listener dies too
    S-->>H: health check fails loudly

stat.label: console commands in the one permanent manual step — every deploy after it is automatic
how long the deploy channel sat unproven while 'manual-in-guest' passed as temporary
new tests proving the predicates and protocol before any real VM was touched

lessons.title: A runtime pinned below a capability its own new feature needs is a version-bridge problem, not a migration problem
lessons.failure: UC-003 Stage C needs the host to open an AF_HYPERV vsock — the Windows socket family for talking to a Hyper-V virtual machine — to the network-less guest where hostile web bytes get parsed. One line in the launcher hard-refused on any interpreter below Python 3.12: if not hasattr(socket, "AF_HYPERV"). The guard was right; what it guarded against was us. The BlarAI runtime venv — its isolated Python environment — is Python 3.11.9, pinned deliberately because OpenVINO GenAI and the 14-billion-parameter model weights are validated against it, and socket.AF_HYPERV first shipped in CPython 3.12. Confirmed on the real venv, not from docs: hasattr returned False. The production interpreter tripped its own guard every time — the runtime was pinned one minor version below a capability one of its own new features needs.
evidence: 2026-06-11 — The capability my own runtime was pinned below
lessons.lesson: The obvious fix — migrate the whole stack to 3.12+ — was rejected hardest: that is re-validating OpenVINO, every pinned wheel, and the draft model that accelerates generation, to buy one socket constant. Hand-reimplementing the socket plumbing was rejected as clever-but-brittle. What shipped is a bridge: the 3.11 launcher spawns a short-lived Python 3.14 helper per operation, the version jump isolated to one process boundary, the runtime venv never touched — and the bridge self-retires the day the runtime catches up.
lessons.growth: Version pins are risk decisions: a runtime validated against specific heavy dependencies will sooner or later lag a capability its newest feature needs, and you will face this fork. Weigh blast radius against frequency — re-validating an entire inference stack to serve a rare control-plane call is the wrong trade — and notice the two seductive wrong exits: the whole-stack migration, and the silent fallback to the old interpreter, which simply recreates the original bug. A bridge done well has three reusable properties: fail-closed discovery, where no qualifying interpreter means a loud refusal and never a quiet degrade; a fast path that makes the bridge self-retiring once the runtime catches up; and a deliberately tiny import surface for the helper, which doubles as a security forcing function. And run your gate on the interpreter the feature actually ships on — that is what made this mismatch visible and reproducible instead of theoretical.
lessons.growth_question: The guard against a missing capability is firing on your own production interpreter — why might migrating the whole stack be the wrong fix, and what is the smallest boundary that can contain the version jump?
lessons.aigp_text: Choosing how to serve one capability without destabilizing a validated runtime is risk treatment, and NIST AI RMF's MANAGE function is exactly that work: prioritizing responses with the rejected alternatives on the record — whole-stack migration and hand-marshalled plumbing both named and declined. The pin itself protects the 'valid and reliable' trustworthy characteristic: the model stack stays on the interpreter it was validated against. Containing the change at one process boundary exemplifies Domain III.C — governing the maintenance of the model and system.
aigp_domains: Domain III — Understanding How to Govern AI Development
aigp_frameworks: NIST AI RMF — trustworthy characteristics (valid and reliable)
lessons.aigp_takeaway: Isolate a needed capability jump at a process boundary; never migrate a validated stack — or silently fall back — for one constant.

no socket.AF_HYPERV"] --> B{"Running interpreter

bridge skipped, self-retired"] B -->|"no"| D["Discover 3.14: config key,

existing tests broken by one new required config field — caught instantly on the real 3.11 venv
